pattern: Counting the digit one up to n is done per place value: for each power of ten, a closed-form formula counts how many times a one appears in that position across all numbers from 0 to n.
hints: Iterating every number to count ones is far too slow near a billion, so count contributions one digit position at a time instead.
Fix a place value (units, tens, hundreds, ...) and ask how many numbers in [0, n] have a one in exactly that position.

Split n at that place into high, current, and low parts: full cycles contribute high * place, and the partial cycle adds 0, the low part plus one, or a full place depending on whether the current digit is 0, 1, or more.

edge_inputs.description: small value covering single and double digit ones
nine has a single one (in the number 1)
a value whose current digit at a place is exactly one
a run of ones across the teens region
a value full of ones tests every place's partial cycle

stress.description: random n across the full allowed range so every digit-length is exercised
stress.note: single argument; optimal closed-form handles the full range instantly while the oracle brute-force is bounded by the input

stress.description: smaller random n that the brute-force oracle can still count digit by digit quickly
stress.note: kept under a few hundred thousand so the O(n) oracle stays well under the time budget
